Cleaning occurrence data
  • stages
  • examples
  • packages

Downloading data

Preparation
Author

Margot, Fonti, Olivia

Published

September 12, 2023

This section copied from download-data.qmd

Once we have decided on our data scope, we can precede to download data. In this section, we will introduce a few common data infrastructures that offer open access biodiversity data and highlight some considerations when choosing one in the context of your data scope.

Throughout this section we will use galah for downloading data. Other options are available.

Choosing a data provider

First, we have to configure galah. This is where you supply your account credentials and set the atlas to a particular region. See ?galah_config for more configuration options.

For this example, we will use data from the (default) Australian node, so we can just provide our registered email address. After that, we will download all records for the Pink Robin.

library(galah)

Attaching package: 'galah'
The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter
galah_config(email = Sys.getenv("ALA_EMAIL"))

pink_robin <- galah_call() %>% 
  galah_identify("Petroica rodinogaster") %>% 
  atlas_occurrences()
arrow::write_parquet(pink_robin, "data/galah/pink_robin")

Filtering rows

Refining your download query ensures higher quality data and also reduces the download size as many data infrastructures impose constraints to download size. We have already seen that it can be valuable to restrict our search to recent records or to particular types of observation. Below we have illustrated how you can refine your query a few quality measures using galah_filter:

tractable_records <- c("LIVING_SPECIMEN", 
                       "PRESERVED_SPECIMEN", 
                       "MATERIAL_SAMPLE", 
                       "MACHINE_OBSERVATION")

pink_robin_tractable <- galah_call() |>
  galah_identify("Petroica rodinogaster") |>
  galah_filter(year > 1945,
               basisOfRecord == tractable_records) |>
  atlas_occurrences()

Searching by region

One way to download data is by filtering to an area of interest using fields already in the ALA and {galah}.

# Show how to download data in galah by finding a field (like by state/territory or smaller region)

Searching by bounding box

Another way to download data is by filtering to return observations within a bounding box.

# Show how to download data in galah with a bounding box

This can be useful when you want to be sure to include records on the border or just outside the border of a defined region (which might affect predictions of where species live in a model).

It can also be useful if you want to filter data yourself.

Selecting specific data columns

By default, atlas_occurrences will return a tibble with a selection of columns containing taxonomic and spatial data as well as other metadata. Alternatively, you can use galah_select to subset the columns that are relevant for your work. To see all available fields you can choose from:

show_all(fields) 
# A tibble: 626 × 4
   id                  description                                   type  link 
   <chr>               <chr>                                         <chr> <chr>
 1 _nest_parent_       <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
 2 _nest_path_         <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
 3 _root_              <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
 4 abcdTypeStatus      ABCD field in use by herbaria                 fiel… <NA> 
 5 acceptedNameUsage   http://rs.tdwg.org/dwc/terms/acceptedNameUsa… fiel… <NA> 
 6 acceptedNameUsageID http://rs.tdwg.org/dwc/terms/acceptedNameUsa… fiel… <NA> 
 7 accessRights        <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
 8 annotationsDoi      <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
 9 annotationsUid      <NA>                                          fiel… <NA> 
10 assertionUserId     User ID of the person who has made an assert… fiel… <NA> 
# ℹ 616 more rows

Here, we will choose a smaller subsets of 8 columns to download for the Pink Robin

project_fields <- c("recordID",
                    "eventDate",
                    "year", 
                    "basisOfRecord", 
                    "occurrenceStatus",
                    "scientificName",
                    "decimalLatitude",
                    "decimalLongitude")

pink_robin_projfields <- galah_call() %>% 
  galah_identify("Petroica rodinogaster") %>% 
  galah_select(project_fields) %>% 
  atlas_occurrences()
arrow::write_parquet(pink_robin_projfields, "data/galah/probin_fields")
read_parquet("data/galah/probin_fields")
